title: I Tried Midjourney 8… Here’s the Truth

segments.text: After a long wait, Midjourney 8 is
finally here and I have some thoughts.
Freepik released a brand new lip-syncing
tool directly inside of their platform
and Adobe is getting into the custom
model game. We have all that and more in
today's episode. Welcome to Curious
Refuge. My name is Caleb. Be sure to
like and subscribe if you want more
videos like this in the future. Okay, so
let's kick things off by talking about
the big news that you probably saw on
social media. Midjourney 8 is finally
here and the team at Midjourney is
saying that this is a big update and
improvement to version 7 that we've had
for quite a while. Specifically, they
said that Midjourney 8 is better at
following details. It's very good at
following style references. The images
that are created are more coherent and
detailed. They're saying that text
rendering is much better inside of
version 8. Images are generated five
times faster and they're introducing an
HD mode that allows you to create 2K
resolution images natively on the
platform. Midjourney has kind of been in
a weird place over the last few months
because in one sense, aesthetically,
Midjourney is really impressive and it
still is one of the better image models
on the market for really conceptually
getting your ideas out there and getting
an image that feels deeply cinematic and
interesting. The problem is details
inside of Midjourney can get just really
weird. You can get too many fingers,
text can be all sorts of wonky, and
altogether, you just begin to notice
kind of strange things inside of
Midjourney. But the promise is
Midjourney 8 is here to solve those
issues. So, I want to hop into the tool,
show you how to use it for yourself, and
then most importantly, we're going to
compare it against Nano Banana. I should
note that Midjourney 8 is in alpha
preview right now, so you may not have
access to this tool just yet. If you go
to alpha.midjourney.com,
you can see if the alpha version is
applied to your account. We've been
subscribed for a while and I think
that's probably why we have access.
Okay, so there are a few things to note.
The first is you'll notice that your
settings are now on the right inside of
this little panel. I think it just helps
to clean up everything and make it feel
like it flows a little better. And then
you'll also notice that for the most
part, it feels very similar to previous
versions of Midjourney. You have your
prompt box at the top. You can add in
your reference images and then you have
the ability to upload mood boards,
personalize and create your own style
using the features that were already
present inside of Midjourney. Okay, so
for our test, let's say a cinematic
still of a man wearing a blue mech suit
in a futuristic market with blue gems.
Now, Midjourney has specifically said
that you may have to discover new ways
to prompt inside of Midjourney 8. They
weren't specific about what those new
ways of prompting are, so we're going to
stick with a classic version of
prompting for the time being. Now, if
you wanted to upload your own style
reference, you do have the ability to
click this little icon here and you can
bring in whatever image or mood board
makes sense for your specific project.
If I'm working on a project that needs a
lot of consistency, I typically will
create a mood board by simply dragging
and dropping images. To do that, all you
have to do is go to mood boards on the
left and then you can basically select a
mood board and drag and drop images to
upload. You can also bring in links from
third-party images as well. Inside of
Midjourney 8, they released a brand new
tag that you should know about and it's
{dash} {dash} HD. And you also have the
ability to select HD mode if you just
click on this little icon here as well.
And you can see we're in Midjourney 8.
So, when you add in {dash} {dash} HD, it
will render it out in a 2K format,
basically giving you more resolution.
Midjourney also released a {dash} {dash}
Q4 tag that supposedly adds in even more
details and quality. They said it will
take four times longer to render out
your images if you use this tag. Okay,
so let's go ahead and render this out
and see what we get. So, inside the old
version of Midjourney, Midjourney
version 7, we got this image here, which
again, aesthetically, looks pretty good.
That is what we were prompting for and
it looks cool. It feels very filmic and
cinematic. I really like the bokeh
details, the color grading. Looks great.
Did a good job. Inside of Midjourney 8,
we got this image here and I'm instantly
confused because the quality is so much
worse than Midjourney 7. I'm not
entirely sure what's going on here, but
version 8 seems to be really not doing a
very good job at all in this specific
instance. We ran the same prompt inside
of Nano Banana Pro and it gave us this
image here, which from a composition
standpoint is closer to what we're
looking for, but it's not as realistic.
And so that's just always a rub whenever
you are going inside of Nano Banana Pro
and I'll talk about why this is the case
in just a little bit. Now, of course,
going into a raw prompt alone and
getting an image in return is one thing,
but you almost always want to use some
sort of style reference to help push
your image generation in a specific
direction. Usually, you will have an
image or a collection of images that are
embodying the color grading, the
composition, and the overall vibe of
what you want your outputs to look like
so they don't come across as
hyper-generic like the image that you're
looking at now. So, for our example, I
got this image here from Unsplash as a
stylistic reference. I really liked the
color grading and I can tell that it's
shot probably on some sort of like Fuji
style camera and I wanted that to be
extended to the images that we
generated. So, I brought our image
inside of Freepik, which is an AI
aggregator. You could work directly
inside of Gemini if you wanted to and I
typed in the exact same prompt, but then
I asked it to use the uploaded reference
image for a style reference to pull
color grading, camera qualities, and
basically, you know, extrapolate that
information and put it inside the final
image. And in return, we got this image
here from Google Nano Banana Pro and I
think it looks fantastic. Is it perfect?
No, but it feels very, very filmic.
There's lots of details here and it has
just that kind of film quality to it
where the details are imperfect, but it
helps to add to the overall realism. So,
I think it did a really, really good
job. Now, to do the exact same thing
inside of Midjourney, all you have to do
is type in your prompt and then go to
the image icon here and I'll select that
original base image that we uploaded
inside of Nano Banana Pro earlier and
you can see it's in here as a style
reference. So, we're good to go. Now,
the one thing I do want to note is you
don't have the ability to bring in style
reference in the HD mode or in the
quality four mode. So, you have to use
the base version of Midjourney 8 and go
ahead and hit render. And after
rendering inside of Midjourney, we had
four images. This is the very best one
and I don't know if it did a very good
job. The character seems very soft and
just details are really just kind of
wonky inside of this image. So, I don't
think it did a very good job. I think
the best way to get quality would be
using Nano Banana Pro with some sort of
style reference. Now, we wanted to test
this out in a variety of different
styles, so let's take a quick look here
at a few other examples. So, we prompted
for a man in a sci-fi film holding a
blue orb that says find the stone and
inside of Midjourney version 7, again,
that's the old version of Midjourney, we
got this image here and you can see it's
okay, but not great. And then of course,
there's just like weird text on the
bottom. It's saying something about Rome
here. Like we're not prompting for that.
The base style does look cinematic, but
the text just looks really, really bad,
frankly. And we prompted inside of
Midjourney 8 and again, it's just bad.
It's just not doing a very good job at
all. Now, compared against Nano Banana
Pro, when we just simply typed in a
prompt alone, we got this generation
here and it is really [music] good,
frankly. It did a really good job. I
like that it says find the stone and it
looks like it's projected. I do think
that the compositing on the text could
be a little improved, but altogether,
it's not that bad and it definitely
gives you something to work off of
because you more than likely would
download this image and then edit it
again inside of Nano Banana. And then we
used the original image as a style
reference with the additional prompt and
it created this image here, which is the
most realistic. I do think it did a
really good job and the compositing on
the text is better even though it is a
little flat towards the camera.
Although, this guy here definitely looks
like some sort of alternate universe
version of Harrison Ford, I'm pretty
sure. So, kind of interesting. And then
using style reference with our prompt
inside of Midjourney 8, we got this
image here, which just looks bad. It
just did not do a very good job. I don't
think the text looks very good and I'm
pretty sure the guy's index finger is
like a little too long. Like there's
just something about it that feels
really, really off. We typed in a prompt
of a man sitting in a coffee shop with
naturalistic lighting and we got this
generation here from Midjourney 7, which
is again, kind of cool, very cinematic.
It's a very unique angle. It doesn't
feel like stock photography. I think it
did a really good job. Inside of
Midjourney 8, we got this image, which
[music] if you look at the guy's face,
it's just really soft and just didn't do
a very good job. And then I think it's
putting way too many cups there in the
background. If he's the customer, the
stack of cups should definitely not be
behind him. And inside of Nano Banana
Pro from a raw prompt, we got this image
here, which definitely seems very
realistic, but I'm beginning to see a
commonality with the images that are
generated inside of Nano Banana Pro.
They all kind of begin to feel the same.
Almost like B-plus stock photography.
They do come across as more
naturalistic, but they still all kind of
feel staged when you're just going into
prompting alone. And using style
reference with Nano Banana Pro, we got
this image here, which I do think is the
most compelling out of all of the
images. There's just simply more
details. I like that there's other
people inside of the coffee shop, and
it's just helping the entire overall
scene to feel a bit more natural. And
then finally, if we compare it against
Midjourney with style reference, we got
this image here, and it's not bad. I
guess it's fine, but like it's trying to
generate a Starbucks logo, and that's
definitely not the Starbucks logo. So,
you would 100% need to run this through
some sort of AI system to help smooth
out everything, in which case you
probably would use Nano Banana Pro or
Magnific, which kind of defeats the
purpose of going to Midjourney alone.
And finally, I wanted to test this out
for a graphic design context, and so I
prompted for a poster that's like a
quirky magazine ad for a space-themed
sushi restaurant. And inside of
Midjourney 7, we got this image, and you
know, it's kind of fun. Seems
hand-drawn. Of course, it gets really,
really weird when you start looking at
the actual text, but at a quick glance,
it looks pretty cool. And this is what
we got from Midjourney 8, which I think
it's pretty awesome. Again, it is a
little quirky, but it definitely is
coming across as like very punk rock,
almost like a band poster. And I think
it did a decent job. Now, compare that
to the same prompt inside of Nano Banana
Pro, and the text here is actually
really good. And what's really funny is
they have this whole cutout here for you
to, I guess, mail in your order for
sushi to this restaurant, which is kind
of funny. And I like just some of the
themes. I like that it turned the sushi
roll into a rocket ship, and you have
this guy riding it. It's It's just kind
of fun. Conceptually, you can tell it's
thinking about things at a higher level.
And I like the little sushi guy just
riding in his little spaceship here.
It's really cute. Now, I also wanted to
test out style reference for a graphic
design example like this. So, I have
this image that was pulled from
Midjourney. I liked the kind of the
overall style, and I used it as style
reference inside of Nano Banana, and
that gave us this image here, and
[music]
it's pretty cool. Like, I think it
really did bring in that style. Of
course, you could dial it up and down as
it makes sense. But altogether, it's
bringing in all of the themes that we're
looking for. I like the aliens as the
sushi chefs here. And again, we have our
little sushi piece driving the alien
spaceship, which is pretty good. It does
come across as a bit clip-arty. It is a
little generic, but altogether, it's not
too bad. And then, this is the result
that we got from Midjourney 8 with that
style reference. And it's funny because
I think if you squint, at a quick
glance, the versions of the images that
you get from Midjourney 8 are better
than Nano Banana Pro. Like, the quality
does come across as more creative and
more interesting, but when you really
start to look at the details, it all
falls apart. And so, you really can't
rely on Midjourney to give you those
high-resolution details that you would
need to use inside a professional
graphic design project. So, with all of
that being said, you might be wondering,
so what's our opinion of using
Midjourney 8? Is it any good for a
creative workflow? And the short answer
is, I think that Midjourney 8 is about
the same as Midjourney 7 when it comes
to the actual utility in a creative
project. You more than likely are not
going to be using Midjourney to generate
a final image that makes it into your
film project or advertising project.
But, I do think it's incredibly valuable
for generating the mood board and the
style references that you will use
inside a smart AI image generator like
Nano Banana Pro. And it all comes down
to the difference between Midjourney and
a lot of the image generators that are
beginning to pop up. Midjourney is more
of a diffusion-based
image generation tool, and I think the
best way to think about it is imagine
that you just threw a bunch of paint
onto a canvas, and you had a system that
went in and just kind of smeared paint
around until it gave you essentially
what you're looking for. It can be
really good at doing finger paints, but
the problem is you're going to run into
some issues when it comes to fidelity
and details because the entire process
is just smearing. It's diffusing and
just kind of mixing noise throughout the
entire canvas. Now, if you compare that
against the way in which tools like Nano
Banana Pro operate, which come from
diffusion transformers, these tools
operate more like Legos. There's
predefined assets that they know need to
make it inside your final image. So, if
you prompt for a man drinking coffee, it
already has a general idea of what a man
drinking coffee should look like, and it
has the intelligence to create the
outline of what that should be inside of
the image. The problem is it can be a
little more generic and less creative in
style because it has a predefined idea
of what that should look like. So,
that's why you almost always need to
bring in a creative style reference into
your diffusion transformers like Nano
Banana Pro to get maximum quality. I
think in the world of AI image
generation, the clock is kind of ticking
for Midjourney. They really need to
update their world model so it has a
more holistic understanding of what is
happening, or I think that just a few
little updates inside of tools like
Google Nano Banana Pro, and the tool is
simply not going to be very helpful
inside a creative context. It's helpful
today, but I could see in a few months
from now that it really could just be
sidelined with all of the innovations
happening around the industry. Now, if
you want to learn more about the process
of AI creativity, you can check out our
free course over at Curious Refuge.
You'll find a link below this video.
Let's move on to talking about the team
at Krea. So, Krea has released their own
mobile app that you can use on your
phone, and it's very similar to other
mobile apps that you've probably used up
to this point. You have the ability to
prompt and have a lot of the tools and
features that you've come to love inside
of Krea on this mobile application.
While this news is not particularly
special, there's all sorts of other
applications from other AI aggregators
and tools on the market. I think it
really does hint that younger
generations are valuing app-based
creativity because they natively started
using creative tools on a smart device
and not on a computer. I think when it
comes to maximum control, you absolutely
need to use a computer because the
workflow is just better, and you can go
through more iterations and keep
everything organized. But the fact that
all of these tools are really viewing
application-based experiences as part of
the future is simply very telling. I
think for any major AI company to stay
relevant and get maximum users on their
platform, you really do need some sort
of mobile application. In other tool
news, the team at Freepik has released a
brand new feature called Freepik Speak.
Essentially, it allows you to take audio
or a script and video and sync up the
lips together. Let me show you how to
use it. To use the tool, all you have to
do is go over to the Freepik website,
and we'll go to video generator. Now,
from here, we'll click on the icon at
the top and select speak. Okay, so you
can bring in a starting image or a
starting video, and it is a little
unclear if this is a proprietary model
inside of Freepik or if this is simply
using third-party tools and they're just
kind of ringing it all together. So,
let's bring in our video here. So, this
is video generated inside of Kling 3.0.
Okay.
Let me go talk to her.
Okay, so you get the idea. Very
emotional scene, but the lips, they
don't really match up. So, let's go
ahead and drag and drop that video into
the video section here. And for audio,
you do have the ability to type in a
script, but I almost always just want to
bring in my own audio. So, we'll go to
the add audio section, and let's drag
and drop our base audio here. Let's take
a quick listen to what that sounds like.
Okay.
>> [sighs]
>> Let me go talk to her. Let's go ahead
and bring that into our audio section.
And when you're ready, go ahead and
click generate. And after a few minutes,
we got this generation here.
Okay.
>> [sighs and gasps]
>> Let me go talk to her.
And the lips look much, much better.
They're not 100%. I'd say they're
somewhere between 90 and 95% of the way
there, but it is interesting. So, there
you go. There's a really quick tool for
doing lip syncing. The team at Adobe has
also just announced Adobe custom models
on their platform. Basically, this gives
you the ability to upload your own
images and train a model. It's similar
to Laura training. Now, the thing that
makes this really interesting is the
fact that this is on Adobe's platform,
which is really billing itself as the
most safe AI model platform out there.
And so, if you work with large companies
or studios, having the ability to upload
secure assets and make sure that no
models are being trained on those pieces
is simply really important. What I
really like about this model is it also
gives you feedback on the assets that
you upload, so you can be ensured that
the very best training data is making it
into the custom model that you're
working on. And the cool part is it's
Adobe as well, which is an aggregator,
and so you'll also be able to generate
inside of other image models using this
custom training data. So, that includes
tools like Nano Banana 2, Google VEO,
and Runway. And then finally, I want to
encourage you to go check out the

You'll find a link below this video.
There were so many good submissions for

We would love to see you at the event.
Okay, I hope you enjoyed this video. As
